narrative: As a Black male applicant with a solid undergraduate GPA of 3.20—right around the typical 3.21 average for students in this dataset—your law school application was evaluated using a predictive model that assesses the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try. The model estimated an 84% probability that you would fail the bar exam, which is high enough to trigger an automatic rejection of your admission to protect the school's bar passage rates and reputation. This prediction was driven primarily by your academic performance in earlier years of schooling, specifically your rankings in Year 1 and Year 3. Your Year 3 school decile of 1—far below the average of about 5.5—stood out as the single biggest factor pushing the model toward predicting failure, as it signals to the model a notable dip in performance relative to peers at a critical stage. Your Year 1 decile of 2, also well below the typical 5.7 average, reinforced this concern as the second-most influential factor, suggesting early struggles that carried weight in the model's eyes. Even your LSAT score of 30, lower than the average 36.7, added to the risk by being the third key contributor toward the failure prediction, despite your full-time student status aligning with the vast majority of applicants. These three elements together outweighed strengths like your GPA and lower-middle family income bracket, painting a picture riskier than for most students who get approved. Compared to typical admitted applicants, your deciles place you in the lower tail of the distribution, where the model sees patterns of bar exam challenges more often. While factors like your race and gender were part of the full profile considered, they didn't shift the outcome here—the academic deciles dominated. This isn't a judgment on your potential or background, but a data-driven flag based on historical patterns. To move forward, focus on retaking the LSAT to boost it toward or above the 36.7 average, and gather evidence of recent academic improvement, like stronger recent grades or targeted bar prep courses, to demonstrate growth. Reapplying after addressing these core areas could significantly improve your odds with similar models or admissions committees. Keep pushing—you've already shown resilience in earning a competitive GPA.
